text.title: Feature Flag Lifecycle
text.summary: Five phases from planning to cleanup; antipatterns that create permanent flag debt.

list.item: Planning: define name, type, owner, expected lifespan; add to registry before writing any code.
Development: implement flag check; write tests for BOTH enabled and disabled paths; deploy with flag OFF.
Rollout: enable for internal users first; increase percentage gradually; monitor error rates and latency.
Full rollout: enable for 100% of users; continue monitoring for at least one full traffic cycle.
Cleanup: remove flag checks from code; delete flag configuration; deploy clean code; mark registry entry as done.

rule.statement: Every feature-flagged code path must have at least one test with the flag enabled and one with it disabled.
rule.rationale: Testing only the happy (enabled) path leaves the disabled path untested. When the flag is cleaned up, the deleted code may have regressions that were never caught.

antipattern.description: Flag debt: releasing flags that are never removed after full rollout.
antipattern.reason: Each permanent flag multiplies code paths. 10 flags create up to 2^10 theoretical combinations; testing all of them is impossible.

antipattern.description: Nested flags: checking flag A inside a block already gated by flag B.
antipattern.reason: Nested flags create an exponential state space. Combinations quickly become untestable and the interaction behavior is unpredictable.

antipattern.description: Using feature flags for configuration values (timeouts, limits, feature counts).
antipattern.reason: Flags are boolean or variant gates. Configuration values belong in environment variables or config files with typed schemas, not flag systems.

text.reference: Registry schema for tracking flag metadata and cleanup dates
